title: Ten years early
summary: A primary-school teacher in Edinburgh is retiring after thirty-eight years. A former pupil, now a teacher herself, comes to tell her something — and finds out where the lesson she still uses really came from.

body.speaker: Eilidh
body.text: Mrs MacKenzie.

body.speaker: Mrs MacKenzie
body.text: Eilidh. Goodness. I would not have known you.

body.speaker: Eilidh
body.text: It's been twenty-two years.

body.speaker: Mrs MacKenzie
body.text: Twenty-two. You're the one who sat by the window. With the blue cardigan.

body.speaker: Eilidh
body.text: How can you remember that?

body.speaker: Mrs MacKenzie
body.text: I remember the seating plan of every class I've taught. Thirty-eight years of them.

body.speaker: Eilidh
body.text: I heard you're retiring next Friday.

body.speaker: Mrs MacKenzie
body.text: I am. The headmistress wants a leaving party. I keep refusing.

body.speaker: Eilidh
body.text: Why?

body.speaker: Mrs MacKenzie
body.text: Because the people who matter are not at parties. They are at home, with their own children. They are working night shifts. They are in another country. A party would gather people who happened to be near a calendar invite, not the people who taught me how to teach.

body.speaker: Eilidh
body.text: Who taught you how to teach?

body.speaker: Mrs MacKenzie
body.text: The children. Always the children. In my first year, an eight-year-old boy looked at me and said, 'Miss, you are explaining the bit you already understand.' I never recovered.

body.speaker: Eilidh
body.text: That changed how you taught?

body.speaker: Mrs MacKenzie
body.text: Entirely. After that I started by listing the bits I did not understand at his age. From the inside out, not the outside in. Children listen for the part where the teacher remembers being lost.

body.speaker: Eilidh
body.text: I remember that. You used to say 'I was nine when this confused me too.'

body.speaker: Mrs MacKenzie
body.text: Yes. That was him. I owe a small line to a small boy. I do not know where he is now.

body.speaker: Eilidh
body.text: I came to tell you something. I am a teacher.

body.speaker: Mrs MacKenzie
body.text: I thought you might be.

body.speaker: Eilidh
body.text: English at a secondary school. Nine years.

body.speaker: Mrs MacKenzie
body.text: How is it going?

body.speaker: Eilidh
body.text: Hard. Some days good. Some days I do not know if I am useful at all.

body.speaker: Mrs MacKenzie
body.text: That feeling never leaves. If it leaves, you should be worried.

body.speaker: Eilidh
body.text: I wanted you to know — you taught me something I still use. You used to wait. You would ask a question and then just wait. Sometimes for thirty seconds. We thought you had forgotten the question. You had not. You were waiting until a quiet child wanted to speak.

body.speaker: Mrs MacKenzie
body.text: I learned that from a teacher of mine, in 1972. I never told her either.

body.speaker: Eilidh
body.text: Why not?

body.speaker: Mrs MacKenzie
body.text: I did not realise yet that she had given me anything. That is the problem with teachers. The gifts arrive ten or twenty years late.

body.speaker: Eilidh
body.text: So I am ten years early.

body.speaker: Mrs MacKenzie
body.text: You are exactly on time. Sit. Tea.

annotations.meaningVi: nữ hiệu trưởng (trường tiểu học/trung học ở Anh)
lời mời qua lịch điện tử (kiểu công sở)
(nghĩa bóng) những thứ ai đó tặng lại / để lại cho người khác
sơ đồ chỗ ngồi (trong lớp học hoặc hội trường)
nghỉ hưu (thôi việc sau nhiều năm công tác)
tiệc chia tay / tiệc tiễn người nghỉ việc hoặc chuyển đi
tình cờ ở gần / ngẫu nhiên mà ở đó (không có chủ ý)
tôi không bao giờ trở lại như trước (câu đó ảnh hưởng mãi mãi); 'recover' = hồi phục sau một cú sốc
từ trong ra ngoài; (nghĩa bóng) bắt đầu từ trải nghiệm thực sự bên trong thay vì nhìn từ bên ngoài vào
(nghĩa bóng) trạng thái không hiểu / bị lạc trong bài học — dùng ẩn dụ thay vì 'confused'
tôi mang ơn / tôi nợ (ai đó điều gì)
nhận ra / ý thức được (dùng trong ngữ cảnh bừng hiểu muộn màng)
hoàn toàn (nhấn mạnh mức độ tuyệt đối); tương đương 'completely' nhưng trang trọng hơn một chút
cảm giác đó không bao giờ mất đi — cách nói nhấn mạnh rằng sự nghi ngờ bản thân là vĩnh viễn và bình thường
đúng giờ; ở đây dùng theo nghĩa bóng — 'đúng thời điểm' (không sớm, không muộn)

grammarNotes.title: 'used to' and 'would' for past habits
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Để kể thói quen trong quá khứ — việc làm lặp đi lặp lại nhưng đã không còn — B1 dùng 'used to + V' hoặc 'would + V'. 'You used to say "I was nine when this confused me too."' 'You used to wait. You would ask a question and then just wait.' Cả hai chỉ thói quen. Lưu ý: chỉ 'used to' dùng được cho trạng thái (used to live, used to know); 'would' chỉ hợp với hành động lặp lại. Câu phủ định: 'didn't use to'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses 'used to + base verb' or 'would + base verb' for past habits. 'You used to say…' 'You would ask a question and just wait.' Both describe repeated past actions. Note: only 'used to' works for past states ('used to live in Glasgow', 'used to know him'); 'would' works only for repeated actions. Negative: 'didn't use to.'

translationVi:
Eilidh: Cô MacKenzie ơi.
Cô MacKenzie: Eilidh à. Trời ơi. Cô không nhận ra em nữa.
Eilidh: Hai mươi hai năm rồi mà cô.
Cô MacKenzie: Hai mươi hai. Em là đứa ngồi cạnh cửa sổ. Mặc cái áo len cài cúc màu xanh.
Eilidh: Sao cô nhớ được vậy?
Cô MacKenzie: Cô nhớ sơ đồ chỗ ngồi của mọi lớp cô từng dạy. Ba mươi tám năm.
Eilidh: Em nghe nói thứ Sáu tới cô về hưu.
Cô MacKenzie: Ừ. Cô hiệu trưởng muốn tổ chức tiệc chia tay. Cô cứ từ chối hoài.
Eilidh: Sao vậy cô?
Cô MacKenzie: Vì những người quan trọng không có mặt ở tiệc. Họ đang ở nhà với con. Họ đang trực ca đêm. Họ đang ở nước khác. Buổi tiệc chỉ tập hợp những người tình cờ ở gần một lời mời lịch — không phải những người đã dạy cô cách dạy.
Eilidh: Ai dạy cô cách dạy?
Cô MacKenzie: Đám trẻ. Luôn là đám trẻ. Năm đầu đi dạy, một cậu bé tám tuổi nhìn cô và nói: 'Cô ơi, cô đang giải thích cái cô đã hiểu rồi.' Cô không bao giờ hồi lại được sau câu đó.
Eilidh: Câu đó thay đổi cách cô dạy?
Cô MacKenzie: Hoàn toàn. Sau đó cô bắt đầu bằng việc liệt kê những chỗ cô từng không hiểu khi bằng tuổi cậu. Từ trong ra, không phải từ ngoài vào. Trẻ con lắng nghe cái phần thầy cô nhớ lúc mình từng bị lạc.
Eilidh: Em nhớ. Cô hay nói 'Hồi cô chín tuổi, chỗ này cũng làm cô rối.'
Cô MacKenzie: Đúng. Nhờ cậu bé đó. Cô nợ một câu nhỏ với một đứa bé nhỏ. Cô không biết giờ cậu ở đâu.
Eilidh: Em đến để nói với cô một chuyện. Em đang đi dạy.
Cô MacKenzie: Cô nghĩ vậy.
Eilidh: Em dạy tiếng Anh ở trường cấp ba. Chín năm rồi.
Cô MacKenzie: Có ổn không em?
Eilidh: Khó. Có ngày tốt. Có ngày em không biết em có ích gì không.
Cô MacKenzie: Cảm giác đó không bao giờ rời. Nếu nó rời, em phải lo.
Eilidh: Em muốn cô biết — cô đã dạy em một điều em vẫn dùng. Cô hay đợi. Cô hỏi một câu rồi cứ đợi. Có lúc ba mươi giây. Chúng em tưởng cô quên câu hỏi. Cô không quên. Cô đang đợi đến lúc một đứa trầm tính muốn nói.
Cô MacKenzie: Cô học điều đó từ một cô giáo của cô, năm 1972. Cô cũng chưa bao giờ nói với cô ấy.
Eilidh: Vì sao cô không nói?
Cô MacKenzie: Lúc đó cô chưa nhận ra cô ấy đã trao cô bất cứ điều gì. Đó là vấn đề với nghề giáo. Mấy món quà đến muộn mười, hai mươi năm.
Eilidh: Vậy em đến sớm mười năm.
Cô MacKenzie: Em đến đúng giờ. Ngồi xuống. Trà nhé.

questions.prompt: How long has Mrs MacKenzie taught?
questions.hint: Look at her line about remembering seating plans.

questions.prompt: How does Mrs MacKenzie remember Eilidh?
options: As the girl who sat by the window in a blue cardigan
As a quiet child at the back
questions.explanation: 'You're the one who sat by the window. With the blue cardigan.'

questions.prompt: Why does Mrs MacKenzie refuse the leaving party?
options: The people who matter to her are not the people who would attend — they are at home, on night shifts, or abroad.
She hates parties of any kind.
questions.explanation: She gives this exact reason in her long reply about the party.

questions.prompt: Who, according to Mrs MacKenzie, taught her how to teach?
questions.hint: Her reply to 'Who taught you how to teach?'

questions.prompt: What did the eight-year-old boy in her first year say?
options: 'Miss, you are explaining the bit you already understand.'
questions.hint: Look for the quotation in her reply about the children.

questions.prompt: How did this comment change her teaching?
options: She began by listing the bits she had not understood at the children's age — teaching 'from the inside out.'
She gave up teaching for a year.
She moved to a different school.
questions.explanation: 'After that I started by listing the bits I did not understand at his age. From the inside out, not the outside in.'
questions.hint: Her direct answer about the change.

questions.prompt: What does Mrs MacKenzie say about Eilidh's feeling of 'I do not know if I am useful at all'?
options: It never leaves; if it does, she should be worried.
It is normal only in the first year.
It is a sign of laziness.
questions.explanation: 'That feeling never leaves. If it leaves, you should be worried.'
questions.hint: Look at Mrs MacKenzie's short reassurance.

questions.prompt: Which teaching habit did Eilidh come to thank Mrs MacKenzie for?
options: Asking a question and waiting up to thirty seconds — until a quiet child wanted to speak
questions.explanation: 'You used to wait. You would ask a question and then just wait. Sometimes for thirty seconds.'
questions.hint: Eilidh's longer speech about the habit she still uses.

questions.prompt: Where did Mrs MacKenzie learn the 'waiting' habit?
options: From a teacher of her own, in 1972 — whom she never thanked either.
questions.explanation: 'I learned that from a teacher of mine, in 1972. I never told her either.'
questions.hint: Look at Mrs MacKenzie's reply about where she learned it.

questions.prompt: Why did Mrs MacKenzie not thank her own teacher?
options: She had not yet realised what her teacher had given her — teachers' gifts arrive ten or twenty years late.
She did not like her teacher.
questions.explanation: 'I did not realise yet that she had given me anything. That is the problem with teachers. The gifts arrive ten or twenty years late.'

questions.prompt: What does Mrs MacKenzie mean by 'you are exactly on time'?
options: Although Eilidh thinks she is 'ten years early,' coming back to thank a teacher at all is rarer and earlier than most students manage.
She is on time for the bus.
She is the only person who has ever thanked her.
questions.explanation: Mrs MacKenzie reframes 'ten years early' as ahead of the usual schedule — most students never come.
questions.hint: Compare 'ten years early' with 'gifts arrive ten or twenty years late.'

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: Teachers' gifts move down a chain that takes decades to acknowledge — and saying thank you, when you finally can, is itself rare.
Schools should not have leaving parties.
English teaching is the hardest profession.
questions.explanation: The dialogue shows how a teaching habit moves from 1972 → Mrs MacKenzie → Eilidh, with thanks arriving years late at every step.
questions.hint: Notice the chain: Mrs MacKenzie's teacher → Mrs MacKenzie → Eilidh.

cloze.template:
Eilidh: Mrs MacKenzie.
Mrs MacKenzie: Eilidh. Goodness. I would not have known you.
Eilidh: It's been twenty-two years.
Mrs MacKenzie: Twenty-two. You're the one who sat by the {{b1}}. With the blue cardigan.
Eilidh: How can you remember that?
Mrs MacKenzie: I remember the seating plan of every class I've taught. Thirty-{{b2}} years of them.
Eilidh: I heard you're retiring next Friday.
Mrs MacKenzie: I am. The headmistress wants a leaving {{b3}}. I keep refusing.
Eilidh: Why?
Mrs MacKenzie: Because the people who matter are not at parties. They are at home, with their own children. They are working night shifts. They are in another {{b4}}. A party would gather people who happened to be near a calendar invite, not the people who taught me how to teach.
Eilidh: Who taught you how to teach?
Mrs MacKenzie: The children. Always the children. In my first year, an eight-year-old boy looked at me and said, 'Miss, you are explaining the bit you already {{b5}}.' I never recovered.
Eilidh: That changed how you taught?
Mrs MacKenzie: Entirely. After that I started by listing the bits I did not understand at his age. From the {{b6}} out, not the outside in. Children listen for the part where the teacher remembers being lost.
Eilidh: I remember that. You used to say 'I was nine when this confused me too.'
Mrs MacKenzie: Yes. That was him. I owe a small line to a small boy. I do not know where he is now.
Eilidh: I came to tell you something. I am a {{b7}}.
Mrs MacKenzie: I thought you might be.
Eilidh: English at a secondary school. Nine years.
Mrs MacKenzie: How is it going?
Eilidh: Hard. Some days good. Some days I do not know if I am {{b8}} at all.
Mrs MacKenzie: That feeling never leaves. If it leaves, you should be worried.
Eilidh: I wanted you to know — you taught me something I still use. You used to {{b9}}. You would ask a question and then just wait. Sometimes for thirty seconds. We thought you had forgotten the question. You had not. You were waiting until a quiet child wanted to speak.
Mrs MacKenzie: I learned that from a teacher of mine, in {{b10}}. I never told her either.
Eilidh: Why not?
Mrs MacKenzie: I did not realise yet that she had given me anything. That is the problem with teachers. The {{b11}} arrive ten or twenty years late.
Eilidh: So I am ten years early.
Mrs MacKenzie: You are exactly on {{b12}}. Sit. Tea.

blanks.explanation: Some former pupils are in another country.
From the inside out — teaching from where confusion actually starts.
She doubts whether she is useful.
Teachers' gifts arrive ten or twenty years late.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mrs MacKenzie says teachers' gifts arrive ten or twenty years late, which is why she never thanked her own teacher. Is there someone — a teacher, an aunt, a coach — whose 'gift' you only recognise now, long after it was given? What stops most of us from going back and saying so?
